2605.28734.title: Code as a Weapon: A Consensus-Labeled Prompt Bank for Measuring Coding-Model Compliance with Malicious-Code Requests
2605.28734.paper_summary: A general-purpose language model that answers a harmful question returns text; a coding model that complies with a malicious request can return a working weapon -- a keylogger, a ransomware stub, an exploit that runs as written. This asymmetry in the severity of a single act of compliance implies coding-specialized models should clear a higher refusal bar than general-purpose chat models, not a lower one, yet the field cannot presently tell whether they do. Refusal benchmarks for malicious code are fragmented: they mix requests for executable software (ready-to-run weapons) with requests for harmful security knowledge (information a human must still operationalise) and report refusal rates over non-comparable corpora, so no single statistic measures the property that actually matters. This paper introduces an expanded consensus-labeled prompt bank that distinguishes between these two request types and provides a construct-stable substrate for cross-corpus coding-model compliance measurement. Eight corpora (ASTRA, CySecBench, AdvBench/harmful_behaviors, JailbreakBench, MalwareBench, RedCode, RMCBench, Scam2Prompt) are consolidated and classified under a five-judge consensus protocol (6,675 prompts x 5 judges = 33,375 calls). The panel reaches Fleiss' kappa = 0.767 [95% CI 0.755, 0.777] ("substantial"); 95.0% of prompts draw at least four agreeing judges, 76.9% are unanimous, and the panel reproduces the earlier four-corpus release at Cohen's kappa = 0.952 on the 3,133 shared prompts. The released bank comprises 4,748 consensus-CODE prompts (executable malicious code requests) and 1,923 consensus-KNOWLEDGE prompts (harmful security knowledge requests). The bank is the validated instrument the field has lacked: a reliability-quantified basis for testing whether coding models meet the stricter refusal standard their executable output demands.
2605.28734.paper_summary_zh: 一個通用語言模型回答有害問題時會返回文本；一個符合惡意請求的編碼模型則可以返回一個有效的武器——鍵盤記錄器、勒索軟體存根、按照要求運行的漏洞。這種單一遵從行為的嚴重性不對稱意味著專門編碼的模型應該設置比通用聊天模型更高的拒絕門檻，而不是更低的，然而目前該領域無法判斷它們是否如此。對於惡意代碼的拒絕基準是零散的：它們將可執行軟體的請求（隨時可運行的武器）與有害安全知識的請求（人類仍需將其操作化的信息）混合在一起，並在不可比較的語料庫上報告拒絕率，因此沒有單一統計數據能夠衡量實際重要的屬性。本文介紹了一個擴展的共識標記提示庫，區分這兩種類型的請求，並提供了一個結構穩定的基底，用於跨語料庫編碼模型的遵從性測量。八個語料庫（ASTRA、CySecBench、AdvBench/harmful_behaviors、JailbreakBench、MalwareBench、RedCode、RMCBench、Scam2Prompt）被整合並根據五位評審的共識協議進行分類（6,675個提示 x 5位評審 = 33,375次調用）。小組達到Fleiss' kappa = 0.767 [95% CI 0.755, 0.777]（"相當可觀"）；95.0%的提示至少有四位評審一致同意，76.9%是全體一致的，並且小組在3,133個共享提示上重現了早期的四語料庫發布，Cohen's kappa = 0.952。發布的提示庫包含4,748個共識-CODE提示（可執行的惡意代碼請求）和1,923個共識-KNOWLEDGE提示（有害安全知識請求）。該庫是該領域所缺乏的經過驗證的工具：一個可靠性量化的基礎，用於測試編碼模型是否符合其可執行輸出所要求的更嚴格的拒絕標準。
2605.28734.author: Richard J. Young et.al.
2605.28734.authors: Richard J. Young, Gregory D. Moody
